1: Chapter 6 opens Job's first formal reply to Eliphaz. The narrative formula 'then Job replied' (wayyaʿan ʾîyôḇ wayyōʾmer) marks each new speech; the book's dialogue structure alternates between a friend's speech and Job's reply in three complete cycles (Job 4-14, 15-21, 22-31) before Elihu (32-37) and God (38-41). The order Eliphaz-Job-Bildad-Job-Zophar-Job is maintained in cycles 1 and 2; in cycle 3 only Eliphaz and Bildad speak before Job's great oath of clearance (Job 31) closes his case.
2: Job opens with a wish that his grief could be weighed on scales (mōʾzĕnayim), directly responding to Eliphaz's implied measurement of Job's sin as the cause of his suffering. The scales/balance metaphor is a standard wisdom motif for divine justice (Proverbs 16:11; Job 11:1; Job 20:23), and Job inverts it: not his sin but his grief should be weighed. The term 'calamity' (hawwāṯî, from hawwāh = ruin/disaster) appears frequently in Job and the Psalms for catastrophic suffering; its weight is literally immeasurable ('heavier than the sand of the sea,' v3).
3: 'Heavier than the sand of the sea' (kāḇēḏ mikḥôl yammîm) — the sand-of-the-sea is the standard OT hyperbole for incalculability (Genesis 22:17; Job 32:12; Hosea 1:10; Revelation 20:8). Job is claiming that his suffering exceeds the capacity of any measurement system — the friends' retributive calculus cannot compute it. The acknowledgment that his 'words have been rash' (lāḵēn ḏĕḇāray lāʿû) is a concession: Job knows his language in Job 3 was extreme, but he attributes it to the weight of his suffering rather than impiety.
4: 'The Almighty's arrows are lodged in me' — the divine-archer imagery draws on both the wisdom tradition and ANE mythology. Psalms 38:2 uses identical imagery: 'your arrows have pierced me'; Psalms 7:13 describes God preparing his weapons against the wicked. The word translated 'venom' (ḥămaṯām = their poison) recalls the poisoned arrows of ancient warfare; the arrows are not merely painful but venomous — they contaminate the wound with additional suffering. 'Terrors of God' (bîʿûṯê ʾĕlôah) — bîʿāh = terror, overwhelm; God's own terrors are personified as an army arrayed against Job, which the military metaphor of 'arrayed against me' (yaʿarĕḵûnî) makes explicit.
5: Job employs animal proverbs to defend the proportionality of his complaints: a wild donkey (pere', the symbol of free, undomesticated nature; Genesis 16:12 applies this word to Ishmael) doesn't bray over fresh grass, and an ox doesn't bellow over its fodder. The ANE proverbial tradition used animal behavior as an argument from nature to establish norms of proportionate response. Job's argument: my complaints are not gratuitous — they are proportionate to genuine deprivation, just as animal cries are proportionate to genuine need.
6: The food proverbs continue: tasteless food without salt, the white of an egg with no flavor. Salt (melaḥ) was the indispensable condiment and food preservative of the ancient world, so scarce in inland areas that it functioned as currency and gave its name to several words (including 'salary' in Latin). 'The white of an egg' (rîr ḥallāmûṯ) is a hapax; translations vary widely. The point is that Job's suffering has made even normal food nauseating — his appetite has been corrupted by the substance of his pain, just as tasteless food is rejected by a healthy appetite.
7: The verse expresses complete sensory revulsion: 'my appetite refuses to touch such things' — the conventional food the friends offer (their theological comfort) is as unpalatable as loathsome food (kĕḏewî laḥmî = like the illness/sorrow of my food). The passage from v5 to v7 moves from the reasonableness of complaint to the total corruption of appetite — Job cannot even receive normal comfort, not because he refuses it but because his suffering has made it impossible to ingest.
8: The optative wish (mî-yittēn = 'O that!') is one of Job's characteristic rhetorical forms: he uses it to express desires that he knows are beyond normal reach but that define what he genuinely needs. Here his request is not healing but death — specifically, to be 'cut off' (yiḇṣāʿēnî = he cuts me off; bāṣaʿ = to cut off, used for breaking threads). The desire for death as relief from unbearable suffering is a recurring theme in the OT: Moses (Numbers 11:15), Elijah (1 Kings 19:4), Jonah (Job 4:3,8), Jeremiah (Job 20:14-18). Job joins this company of those whose suffering reached the limit of endurance.
9: The request that God 'crush me, let loose his hand and cut me off' — the three verbs (crush = yĕdakkĕʾēnî, let loose = yatter, cut off = yiḇṣāʿēnî) describe a comprehensive divine act of termination. The theological significance is that Job is asking God to be the agent of his death, not anyone else. This is not suicidal despair but theodicy: if God is the one who has brought this suffering, let God also be the one who ends it. The request for divine termination rather than human comfort frames death as the only adequate divine response to divinely inflicted suffering.
10: 'I have not concealed the words of the Holy One' (lōʾ kihhadtî ʾimrê qāḏôš) — Job's ground of consolation even in death would be his consistent integrity in not suppressing or denying what God's word/will demanded of him. kāḥaḏ (to hide/deny by concealment) appears in Joshua 7:19 for Achan's hiding of sin; Job uses its negative: he has not hidden his acknowledgment of God's word. The 'Holy One' (qāḏôš) is the title used by Isaiah for YHWH (Isaiah 1:4; Job 5:19 'the Holy One of Israel'), giving Job's confession of faithfulness a specifically monotheistic grounding.
11: The two questions — 'what is my strength, that I should hold on?' (mah-kōḥî kî ʾăyaḥēl) and 'what do I have to look forward to, that I should endure?' (mah-qiṣṣî kî ʾaʾărîḵ nap̄šî) — establish the limits of human endurance. Strength (kōaḥ) and future-orientation (qēṣ = end/goal) are the two foundations of perseverance; without either, endurance loses its ground. This is not complaint but honest diagnosis: what resources are available to keep going? The verse anticipates the NT's reframing of endurance as possible only with external strengthening (2 Corinthians 12:9; Philippians 4:13; Hebrews 12:1-3).
12: The stone/bronze simile for impossible endurance: stone (ʾāḇen) is the OT symbol for hardness and permanence (Job 38:30; Ezekiel 11:19 where God promises to replace the 'stone heart' with a 'heart of flesh'); bronze (nĕḥûšāh) is hard, unyielding metal. Job's rhetorical question implies that endurance requires inhuman material composition — he is not stone or bronze, he is flesh, and flesh has limits. The frailty-of-flesh theme runs through Job (Job 4:19; Job 10:4-9; Job 14:1-2) and connects to the NT's consistent acknowledgment that human weakness requires divine strength (2 Corinthians 4:7-9; Hebrews 4:15).
13: 'Is there no help left in me? Has all sound judgment been driven away from me?' — the verse registers the total depletion of internal resources: ʾên-ʿezrāṯî ḇî (no help is in me), tûšiyyāh (sound wisdom/effective capability, the distinctive Joban term; see Job 12:16; Job 26:3) niddĕḥāh mimmenî (driven from me, as if scattered by a storm). Total internal depletion is the condition under which Job makes his complaint — not from abundance of options but from their complete exhaustion.
14: 'The despairing person deserves steadfast love from a friend' (lammās mērēʿēhû ḥāseḏ) — this verse is theologically significant because it applies ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty/steadfast love, the term for YHWH's faithful relationship with Israel: Exodus 34:6-7; Ps 136) to the horizontal relationship between human friends. ḥeseḏ as a social obligation between humans appears in Ruth 3:10 (Ruth's ḥeseḏ toward Naomi); Genesis 21:23 (covenantal ḥeseḏ between humans); Proverbs 19:22. 'To withhold it is to forsake the fear of the Almighty' — the friends' failure of ḥeseḏ toward Job is simultaneously a failure of their theology: proper fear of God should produce ḥeseḏ toward his image-bearer in distress.
15: 'My brothers are as treacherous as a seasonal brook' — the wadi metaphor begins. The wadis of the Negev and Sinai are intermittent streams that flow only during winter rains and completely dry up in summer. As a metaphor for unreliable friends, the wadi is apt: it appears when least needed (in the cold season when water is not scarce) and disappears when most needed (in the heat and drought). The treachery (ḇāgad = to act treacherously, the word for covenant-betrayal in Proverbs 2:22; Malachi 2:14-16) of the friends is like the wadi's disappearance: predictable given the conditions, but devastating to those who counted on the water.
16: The technical description of the wadi's winter flow: darkened by melting ice (qādēr min-qaraḥ = dark from ice), swollen where snow lies hidden (yitʿallēm-ʿālaw šāleg = snow hidden/covered upon it). The semi-arid Sinai and Negev regions do receive occasional winter snow; the melting contributes to the winter wadi-flow that creates the paradox: the stream is fullest in the season when water is least needed for survival.
17: The disappearance in summer: 'when the heat comes they vanish' (ḇĕʿēṯ zārĕbû niṣmāṯû = in the time they are scorched, they are cut off). niṣmāṯû (from ṣāmat = to silence/cut off; also used in Psalms 94:23 for God cutting off the wicked). The summer heat that makes water most critical is precisely when the wadi 'dries up from its channel' (yidʿăkû mimqômām). The seasonal pattern of the unreliable wadi mirrors the friends' behavior: present when Job was prosperous (Job 29), absent now in his extremity.
18: 'The courses they ran turn aside; they rise into wasteland and vanish' (yilpĕṯû ʾorḥôṯ darqām yĕhalikû ḇatōhû wayyōʾḇēḏû). The word tōhû (wasteland/formlessness, the primordial void of Genesis 1:2) is applied to the empty space into which the wadi's waters disappear — literally evaporating into nothingness. The use of the creation-language word tōhû for the friends' destination gives their disappearance cosmic resonance: the unreliable friends return to the formless void that preceded creation.
19: Tema and Sheba are historically identifiable Arabian locations. Tema (modern Tayma) is an oasis city in northwest Arabia (Isaiah 21:14; Jeremiah 25:23; Ezra 2:53) that controlled major caravan routes through the northern Arabian desert; it was significant enough for Nabonidus (Babylonian king) to relocate there for a decade (556-539 BCE). Sheba (in southern Arabia, modern Yemen) was the source of gold, spices, and incense that traveled north through Arabia on the incense route (1 Kings 10:1-13, the Queen of Sheba; Isaiah 60:6; Psalms 72:10). The caravans of these two major trade centers looking for water makes the wadi metaphor geographically precise: actual trading parties died when expected water sources failed.
20: 'They are put to shame because they trusted them' (ḇōšû kî-ḇāṭāḥ = they are ashamed because they trusted) — bāṭaḥ (to trust/rely on) is the standard vocabulary for faith/trust (Psalms 22:4-5; Proverbs 3:5; Isaiah 31:1; Jeremiah 17:5-8); its application to the caravan's misplaced trust in the wadi explicitly parallels the problem of trusting unreliable comfort. 'They come and find themselves disappointed' — the travelers' experience of arriving at a dry wadi to find nothing is the exact experience of Job encountering his friends: he came hoping for water and found emptiness.
21: 'You have become just like them' — Job makes the application of the wadi metaphor explicit. The shift to second person (lî hĕyîṯem lōʾ = you have become nothing to me) is a direct accusation. 'You see my disaster and shrink back in fear' — the friends' withdrawal is not merely unhelpful but is an active display: they are afraid (tîrāʾû = you are frightened) at the sight of Job's suffering. The fear-response to suffering in one's friend is a theme in the Psalms (Job 38:11; Job 88:8,18: 'my companions avoid me because of my wounds').
22: Job denies making any financial requests of the friends: he has not asked for a 'gift' (šōḥaḏ = a bribe or present; the word has both neutral and pejorative senses — a gift to influence, cf. Proverbs 17:8,23; Job 18:16) from their wealth. The question is rhetorical: of course he hasn't. The verse establishes that Job's needs are not material but relational and theological — he wants truth (v25-26), not money. The absence of financial demands makes the friends' silence and inadequate response all the more inexcusable.
23: The two further negatives — 'save me from my enemy's hand' (pĕlṭûnî = rescue me), 'rescue me from the grip of the ruthless' (ûmiyyad ʿārîṣîm tippĕḏûnî = ransom me from the hand of the violent) — establish that Job has asked for none of the normal things one asks friends for: money, legal defense, protection from enemies. His need is categorically different from the standard social obligations of friendship, yet the friends still fail him at the most basic level.
24: 'Teach me and I will be quiet' (hôrûnî wăʾănî ʾaḥărîš) — Job's offer is a genuine invitation: if the friends can actually demonstrate his sin, he will accept their conclusion in silence. 'Show me where I have gone wrong' (ûmah šāgîṯî hăḇînû lî = and where I have erred, make me understand). The vocabulary of teaching (yārāh = to instruct/teach, the root of tôrāh), quietness (ḥāraš = to be silent), and understanding (bîn = to discern) frames Job's request in precisely the terms of wisdom-instruction. He is not refusing wisdom; he is demanding that the friends actually deliver it.
25: 'How compelling honest words are!' (mah-nîmrĕṣû millîm-yōšer = how forceful/painful are words of uprightness!) — the irony is that the friends' words are not honest: they are using the standard wisdom vocabulary (v27) while misapplying it to Job's situation. 'But what does your argumentation prove?' (ûmah-yôḵîaḥ hôḵēaḥ mikkkem = and what does reproof from you prove?) — the forensic question yôḵîaḥ / hôḵîaḥ (from yāḵaḥ = to reprove/argue) returns us to the legal metaphor. The friends' arguments are formally correct but prove nothing because they rest on a false premise (Job's guilt).
26: 'Do you intend to reprove words, as if the speech of a despairing man were just wind?' — lĕhôḵîaḥ millîm taḥšĕḇû (do you think to reprove words alone?); ûlĕrûaḥ ʾimrê nōʾāš (and the words of a desperate man are wind?). The dismissal of suffering-language as 'mere words' or 'just wind' is the theological move Job is challenging: the friends are treating his complaints as formally invalid (wind = rûaḥ, meaningless breath) rather than as genuine testimony from someone in genuine distress. This mirrors the dismissal of lament in some religious communities: that mourning and protest are signs of inadequate faith.
27: 'You would cast lots over an orphan and sell out your friend' — the accusation escalates: the friends' callous attitude toward Job's suffering is equivalent to the most extreme forms of exploitation (casting lots = gambling over a helpless person's fate; yattîlû ʿal-yāṯôm = you throw lots upon an orphan). The orphan (yātôm) is the paradigmatic figure of social vulnerability whose protection is the test of societal justice throughout the OT (Exodus 22:22; Deuteronomy 10:18; Psalms 68:5; Isaiah 1:17; James 1:27). The comparison says: the friends treat Job the way a heartless gambler treats an orphan.
28: 'Be willing to look me in the face' — the appeal for direct, honest engagement: p̄ĕnû-nāʾ ʾēlay wĕʿal-p̄ānêḵem ʾim-ʾăḵazzēḇ (turn to me; will I lie to your faces?). The face-to-face encounter is the mark of authentic relationship in the OT: YHWH speaks to Moses 'face to face, as a man speaks with his friend' (Exodus 33:11); Jacob names Peniel 'the face of God' after surviving his encounter (Genesis 32:30). The friends who won't look Job in the face are refusing the most basic form of human-divine encounter: honest presence.
29: 'Come back now; let there be no injustice here' — šûḇû-nāʾ ʾal-tĕhî ʿawlāh = return please; let there be no iniquity. šûḇ (to return/repent) is the standard word for both physical return and theological repentance; here Job uses it to call the friends back to him, back to honest engagement. 'My integrity is at stake' (wĕšûḇ ʾôḏ ṣiḏqî-ḇāh = and turn back again — my righteousness is in it); Job's ṣeḏeq (righteousness/integrity) is at stake in this encounter. The word ṣeḏeq used of Job himself will become one of the book's central contested terms.
30: Job closes his reply by asserting the reliability of his own moral sense: 'is there any wrongdoing on my tongue?' (hăyēš-bĕlišônî ʿawlāh = is there iniquity on my tongue?) and 'can my own sense of taste not tell what is harmful?' (ʾim-ḥiḵḵî lōʾ-yāḇîn hawwôṯ = does not my palate perceive disasters?). The palate-as-discernment metaphor (Job 12:11; Job 33:2; 34:3 for ear/palate testing words) closes the chapter: Job has the same capacity for moral discernment that the friends have, and he asserts it is working correctly. His complaint is not the raving of an impaired moral sensor but the accurate report of a properly functioning one.
